skill.name: go-mod-helper
skill.description: Guides Go module setup, dependency updates, private module access, workspaces, vendoring, CI checks, and release preparation.
skill.summary: Manage Go modules, dependencies, workspaces, and private repository configuration.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, script, filesystem, network, and credential patterns in SKILL.md. Most are Markdown examples for normal Go module workflows, but the skill includes credential persistence and global configuration guidance that can expose tokens or alter user environments if executed without review. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration intent was found.

medium_findings.title: Credential Persistence Guidance for Private Modules
medium_findings.description: The skill shows creating ~/.netrc with a GitHub token, using ssh-add on a private key, enabling credential.helper store, and embedding CI_JOB_TOKEN into a Git URL rewrite. These are legitimate private module patterns, but they can expose credentials or persist secrets if used without explicit user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The credential-related lines are explicit and semantically relevant to private module access. I found no evidence that the skill exfiltrates these credentials, so the issue is unsafe guidance rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Global Go and Git Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends go env -w and git config --global commands for GOPRIVATE, GONOPROXY, GONOSUMDB, GOFLAGS, and URL rewrites. These can change user-wide module resolution, proxy use, checksum behavior, and Git transport settings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands clearly target persistent Go and Git configuration. The intended use is legitimate, but running them automatically would have broad effects outside the current repository.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Flags Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The numerous shell and Makefile command detections are inside fenced Markdown examples for Go module, build, test, and release workflows. They do not indicate hidden executable skill code, but agents should still request confirmation before running mutating commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is fenced Markdown documentation, not a script file or tool implementation. Some commands are mutating, so the pattern is not entirely irrelevant.

low_findings.title: Go import and require Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: Dynamic import and require detections correspond to Go import blocks and go.mod require blocks, not JavaScript dynamic imports or runtime module loading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are visibly Go and go.mod syntax in fenced examples. There is no JavaScript execution context or user-controlled dynamic loader.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Lack Supporting Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm detections do not map to cryptographic API calls in the reviewed Markdown context. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or similar weak hashing being recommended for security use.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are descriptive text, file names, package comments, or build metadata examples. I did not find semantic evidence of weak cryptographic guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Plaintext Credential File Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The HTTPS authentication example writes a GitHub token into ~/.netrc. This is a known private module pattern, but it persists a secret on disk and should be scoped, protected, and approved by the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly writes login and password fields to ~/.netrc. The mitigation chmod is present, but the secret still persists in a credential file.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI Token in Global Git URL Rewrite
dangerous_patterns.description: The GitLab CI example places CI_JOB_TOKEN inside a global Git URL rewrite. This can leak into process listings, logs, or persisted Git configuration if copied outside the intended CI scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token interpolation is explicit and affects Git configuration. The context is CI documentation, so this is risky guidance rather than confirmed secret theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Private Module Checksum and Proxy Changes
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents GONOPROXY and GONOSUMDB changes for private module paths. This is expected for private modules, but broad patterns can reduce checksum verification and proxy protections beyond the intended repositories.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are valid Go environment configuration. Risk depends on the breadth of the user-supplied patterns, so confidence is high for the pattern and moderate for exploitability.

content.value_statement: Go module work often fails because dependencies, private repositories, and release tags are configured inconsistently. This skill gives structured guidance for go.mod, go.sum, workspaces, vendoring, CI checks, and private module setup.
actual_capabilities: Explains go.mod and go.sum structure with module, require, replace, exclude, and retract examples.
Provides common go get, go mod tidy, go mod download, go mod verify, and go list workflows.
Guides private module setup with GOPRIVATE, GONOPROXY, GONOSUMDB, SSH, and CI examples.
Shows workspace mode, vendoring, build constraints, and multi-module repository patterns.
Offers troubleshooting steps for checksum mismatches, module resolution failures, version conflicts, and cache issues.
Includes CI, testing, build, release, and semantic versioning checklists for Go modules.
limitations: It is documentation-only and does not inspect a repository unless the agent reads project files separately.
Some examples change global Go or Git settings and need user approval before execution.
Private module guidance uses placeholder credentials and must be adapted to local security policy.
It does not replace dependency vulnerability scanning or license compliance review.

use_cases.title: Initialize a Go Project
use_cases.description: Create a new module, choose a module path, set up common folders, and add first dependencies.

use_cases.title: Fix Dependency Problems
use_cases.description: Resolve checksum mismatches, version conflicts, missing modules, and corrupted module cache issues.

use_cases.title: Prepare Private Modules for CI
use_cases.description: Plan GOPRIVATE, proxy, checksum, Git, and CI settings for private Go dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Module
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-mod-helper to initialize a Go module for this project. Explain the module path choice and the first go mod commands.

prompt_templates.title: Clean Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-mod-helper to review my Go dependencies. Suggest tidy, verify, update, and go.sum maintenance steps before I commit.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot Private Modules
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-mod-helper to diagnose private module access failures. Check GOPRIVATE, proxy, checksum, SSH, and CI credential settings.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use go-mod-helper to design a release workflow for a multi-module Go repository with workspaces, CI verification, tags, and vendoring rules.

output_examples.input: I need to add a dependency and keep go.mod clean.
output: A short plan for adding the dependency at the intended version.
A cleanup sequence using tidy, download, and verify checks.
A reminder to review go.mod and go.sum before committing.

output_examples.input: My private GitHub module cannot be downloaded in CI.
output: A checklist covering GOPRIVATE, proxy bypass, checksum bypass for private paths, and Git authentication.
A warning to avoid storing long-lived tokens in global configuration.
A CI-focused path that keeps credentials scoped to the job.

output_examples.input: Prepare my module for a v2 release.
output: Guidance to update the module path with the v2 suffix.
Checks for imports, tags, documentation, tests, and release notes.
A final verification sequence before publishing the tag.

best_practices: Review commands that change global Go or Git settings before running them.
Run tests, go mod tidy, and go mod verify before committing dependency changes.
Use scoped credentials for private modules, especially in CI environments.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real tokens into shared prompts, files, or persistent global Git configuration.
Do not disable checksum or proxy behavior broadly when only private module paths need exceptions.
Do not run cache cleanup or dependency updates without checking current repository changes first.

faq.question: Does this skill run Go commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides command examples and guidance. An agent may run commands only when the user asks and the environment allows it.

faq.question: Can it help with private Go modules?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers GOPRIVATE, proxy settings, checksum settings, SSH, token-based access, and CI configuration.

faq.question: Is the credential guidance safe?
faq.answer: It needs caution. Some examples store credentials or alter global settings, so users should prefer scoped and temporary credentials.

faq.question: Can it update dependencies?
faq.answer: It can guide dependency updates with go get, go mod tidy, go mod download, and go mod verify workflows.

faq.question: Does it detect vulnerable dependencies?
faq.answer: No. It recommends auditing dependencies, but it does not include a vulnerability database or scanner.

faq.question: Can it support multi-module repositories?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Go workspace mode, replace directives, vendoring, and CI patterns for multi-module projects.
